content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. A movie depicting a healthy/happy relationship

**Score:** 20
**Body:** Couples in movies seem to always fight! Can you find me a movie where

* The main characters are a romantic couple
* They are happy together and don't fight (at all)
* The movie's conflict comes from some external source, not the relationship itself
* The couple works together as a team to address the problem

Please no superheroes or children's films.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Addams Family Values
2. If you enjoy classics, The Thin Man movies are excellent examples of this. They’re cozy detective stories where the main characters are a happily married couple, peppered with amusing and smart dialogue. 
3. My Big Fat Greek Wedding 
4. The Mummy is one of my favorite examples of this. Rick and Evie have disagreements and teasing, but they're never really at odds with each other. Once they're together, the conflict is entirely external and they work as a team the whole way through
5. Date Night 
6. About time 
7. Game night
8. it’s been a while since i saw it- so maybe i’m wrong!? -but maybe Away We Go (2009)? if anyone has seen and knows i’ve misremembered, lemme know! 
9. The parents in Easy A
10. I seem to remember the Sam Mendes film Away We Go being like this. 

## 2. Movies about people who are having a serious mental break like The Machinist or Taxi Driver. Or have a serious mental illness like Schizophrenia. With minimal to no romance though

**Score:** 17
**Body:** Preferably main character is having a very serious mental break and are losing their mind. I’m also interested in movies where the main character has some sort of serious mental illness like schizophrenia. But idk any examples off the top of my head. 

I’m chill with reading subtitles. I hate romance movies so none of that. I don’t usually like straight up action or comedies but if they’re really good that’s okay. I tend to prefer movies from the 70s/80s/00s and the 2020s so far but anything is okay. Thank you 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Clean, Shaven (1993)

Pi (1998)

Possession (1981)

Take Shelter (2011)
  - Second *Clean, Shaven*. Just a harrowing depiction of psychosis. 
2. Memento, A Beautiful Mind, Nightcrawler, Sucker Punch, The Voices, Spider, Being John Malkovich, The Truman Show, Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind, Colossal
3. They Look Like People 
4. girl, interrupted 
5. Fight Club (1999) with Brad Pitt and Edward Norton
6. Clockwork Orange
7. Jacob’s Ladder
8. I wouldn’t call it romantic but I guess there are threads of it. Bug is incredible. Michael Shannon’s acting is so spot on. My dad was mentally ill and I could see him in Shannon’s face. I will not be watching it again because it messed with my head but it’s good. 
9. Perfect Blue (1997)

>*Perfect Blue follows Mima, a pop singer who transitions into acting. As she takes on her new career, her sense of reality begins to fracture when she is stalked by an obsessive fan. Mima starts to feel that her life is no longer fully her own, as the boundary between fact and illusion blurs. Fearing for her safety, she must struggle to distinguish what is real in order to survive.*

## 3. Action movies with very very good fight scenes

**Score:** 14
**Body:** I just watches The Raid and Hard Boiled so I'm craving action movies with very good fight scenes. I would prefer ones that have very long fight sequences in them too. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. THEY LIVE
2. Ong Bak
3. Kung Fu Hustle
4. Shoot 'Em Up (2007)
5. Atomic Blonde is spectacular.
Eastern Promises if you can handle it
6. The Matrix...The Highway scene in Reloaded in particular 
7. Monkey Man if you’re looking for more brutal and messy fight scenes. 
8. Jackie Chan's films have the most epic Fights...Tony Jaa as well
9. Rumble in the Bronx might be the most fun 
10. The Kingsmen (first one) has a top 5 fight scene of all time.

Boy kills world 

Sisu



## 4. Older Live action for young kids

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Mary Poppins, and Bedknobs and Broomsticks were well received. The Sound of Music was Too slowly paced. The Wizard of Oz was too scary. 

Edit: kids are younger than 5 if that helps.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Muppet Movie (1979)
  - Any of the muppet movies!
    - They are! I keep thinking of "older" movies being from the 1960s or older, so I didn't include them in the list of what my kids have already seen. They haven't seen treasure Island or the Manhatten one yet.
2. The Parent Trap, Cheaper by the Dozen, Mrs Doubtfire, Big, Beethoven, Flubber, Hook, Matilda, Practical Magic, The Secret Garden.

It's been a while since I've watched any of these but I remember liking them as a kid!
3. I loved Pete's Dragon (1977) directed by Don Chaffey when I was a kid.

  
ETA: also the Herbie movies, the first one is The Love Bug (1968) directed by Robert Stevenson.
4. Chitty Chitty Bang Bang, Heidi starring Shirley Temple
5. Parent Trap (1961)

Flight of the Navigator

Savannah Smiles

 Homeward Bound

Annie 

Chitty Chitty Bang Bang

Beethoven 

The Love Bug (1968)

Swiss Family Robinson

Flipper

Shirley Temple movies

That Darn Cat!

Anne of Green Gables

Pollyanna
6. Neverending Story.
7. For a hybrid, Who Framed Roger Rabbit. 
8. Paddington movies.  I don’t know if parts will be scary (for instance the villain trying to capture Paddington)

## 5. Films where we don't know much of what's going on

**Score:** 14
**Body:** It's sort of difficult to explain, but I'm looking for movies where not everything that is going on gets explained or revealed, but you clearly still get a feeling that something bigger is going on. For example, maybe movies where we only follow the protagonists so the audience only knows as much as they do and only learns new stuff if they figure it out or think they have figured it out. Something that really gives you a helpless feeling of how little you actually know about everything that is going on, and a lot of things are up to speculation or maybe even a case where the protagonists we follow have very little control over everything that is happening. We can learn some new stuff along the way or be given some limited clues, but even at the end I would prefer if there still remain a lot of things or at least some things we still don't know so not everything gets revealed or explained. Thanks.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Mulholland Drive - it's 100% what you're looking for
2. Primer
3. Picnic at Hanging Rock (1975)
4. Memento
5. The Conversation starring Gene Hackman 
6. Caddo Lake
7. The Wailing.
8. Memento

Cube (1997)

Shutter Island

Stay (2005)

Pale Blue Eye

Synecdoche, New York (a lot of the director's films are cryptic and winding like this)
9. **Cure** by Kurosawa – no not that Kurosawa, the young one.

Later you can join us all in arguing about what the heck we all thought that was about. It’s very satisfying nonetheless.

**Piaffe** definitely is like that!

10. Solaris

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What are your thoughts on Sam Rockwell? I think he's one of those rare actors who never really disappoints. Few actors can make eccentric, unconventional characters feel as believable and relatable as he does.

**Score:** 1776
**Body:** What's your favorite supporting performance by Sam Rockwell in terms of pure entertainment value?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|up4Acmw3vCCOJaZus7|downsized)
  - Galaxy Quest is always the right answer
    - That was my first thought. Why was Galaxy Quest excluded? One of his most memorable roles. 
  - he stole every scene he was in, in a film that also starred Alan Rickman - that's a sign of brilliance
2. Zaphod Beeblebrox is one of my favorites characters.
3. "Regular or Menthol?"

https://preview.redd.it/4nhn7bylw24h1.jpeg?width=1280&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=425abaea20c46f6056cb299d9e5f6b20cf676c3c
4. https://preview.redd.it/2rdz5c48v24h1.jpeg?width=640&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=4b2e8e45e92f133785fd18f66da1285b11663ebb

Amazing actor, always steals the movie. Loved him in seven psychopaths
5. You forgot one.

https://preview.redd.it/i7sby0fcv24h1.jpeg?width=374&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=81209f341fcd6e9fd19cb17eb59d438dd7cc6333
6. Zaphod Beeblebrox. Him as the president of the galaxy in the Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy still cracks me up. I couldn't picture anyone else in that roll.

https://preview.redd.it/6buqgpegy24h1.jpeg?width=277&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=a9652bd2ced9360781da3636be87cd34bf1f2a44
7. Sam Rockwell is a national treasure. For pure entertainment, it's gotta be Justin Hammer in Iron Man 2. Dude showed up, stole every scene with his awkward dancing and terrible charisma, and somehow made a weapons dealer fun to watch. Also loved him in Seven Psychopaths.

## 2. The Breadwinner (2026) Unfunniest movie of the year contender

**Score:** 1667
**Body:** Saw The Breadwinner last night and I'm really struggling to see how Nate Bargatze would think this was even remotely funny. Clearly steals character arcs and plot points from movies like Daddy's Home and Cheaper by the Dozen only to make them significantly less funny. Anyone else seen this yet?

Full review for those interested [https://adamreviewsfilm.com/the-breadwinner/](https://adamreviewsfilm.com/the-breadwinner/)

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. An answer to the age old question “What if we took the commercial where the dad doesn’t know which laundry detergent to use and made it into a feature film?”
  - Its called "Mr. Mom" and it came out in 1983.
    - And it’s great 
    - Well, Nate Bargatze is no Michael Keaton.
      - No one but Michael Keaton and all of his clones from Multiplicity is Michael Keaton
  - And yet they won’t make the Foger’s coffee commercial into a feature length film!
    - Yes they did. I've watched it a bunch. It's on pornhub.
  - Oh that's good. 

Well said
2. The most scathing review I've heard is "This would have been a fantastic Netflix movie."
3. Meh - Michael Keaton is the King of Stay at Home Dads

## 3. What’s the funniest unscripted movie moment ever?

**Score:** 1079
**Body:** For me, it’s when the old dude’s dentures slip when he’s singing at Connie Corleone’s wedding in The Godfather (1972).

He had so much onstage swag that, even after the slip, he recovered nicely to finish the verse. lol

What are some other hilarious examples of spontaneous bloopers that made it into the final cut of a film?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. In “It’s A Wonderful Life” when Uncle Billy is drunk outside the Bailey home, someone on the crew off camera dropped something and it made a crash. The actor that played Uncle Billy played it as though he fell in to a trash can off screen since he was drunk. “I’m all right, I’m all right!”
2. Blazing Saddles.

These are people of the land.  The common clay of the new West.

You know . . . _morons_

Cleavon Little's reaction was real!

Yeah, that's my fav!
3. Zoolanders "But why male models" 
Ben Stiller forgot his line and just repeated his last one, and David Duchovny "are you serious, I just told you" gets me every time 
  - I think David Duchovny also deserves a lot of credit for staying in character and continuing the improv. 
4. ![gif](giphy|12jHPvCe8Nh87C)

Paul Rudd was on Conan’s podcast  recently and said this scene was basically entirely improvised, and Paul was trying to say things to make Will Ferrel break, but Will being the master that he is, just said in character “That doesn’t make any sense” and it stayed in the film.
5. Billy Crystal and Carol Kane apparently ad-libbed much of the Miracle Max scene in The Princess Bride. 
  - Rob Reiner had to leave the set because he kept screwing up the takes with laughter.
    - And Cary Elwes cracked a rib trying to suppress his laughter since he was mostly dead.
6. One of the most well done probs was in Dr. Strangelove or: How I Learned to Stop Worrying and Love the Bomb - when George C Scott is in the war room talking with the President, and he turns to walk back towards the war room table table and completely falls, trips, stands up and keeps on doing his lines without missing a beat. I’ve always understood that it was one of the most famous seamless transitions of an unscripted mistake that didn’t land on the cutting room floor.
7. ![gif](giphy|7cTTE2Z1OmrFm)

Gary Oldman deliberately overacted this take to make the director laugh. He never expected it to make the final cut.

Edit: the film is “Léon: The Professional”.

## 4. What’s a film that basically works entirely because of the excellent chemistry between the leads?

**Score:** 582
**Body:** Rewatched “What’s Your Number?” recently and the basketball scene kinda made me realise why I even decided to rewatch it in the first place lol. It’s just a silly little regular romcom otherwise that doesn’t offer anything fresh, but I think their chemistry is beautiful and so natural in this film. The entire film is basically rewatch-worthy for me personally solely because of their amazing chemistry. 

So what are your picks for films that are almost entirely carried by the chemistry between the leads? 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|qL6klV4uzqiQ)
  - In a just universe, The Nice Guys 4 would be out now. 
2. Overboard is a horror movie without the infinite chemistry and charm of Goldie Hawn and Kurt Russell. It in no way should function yet against all logic and reason it does
3. ![gif](giphy|HfqxbYdhij0qc)
4. The Banshees of Inisherin
  - And by extension; In Bruges
5. The forgotten Christmas action movie The Long Kiss Goodnight. Samuel L Jackson and Gena Davis were incredible together on screen.
6. The "Before" Trilogy

![gif](giphy|9zt4tLxLtP7Es)
7. ![gif](giphy|125uCGIGCsU1dm)
8. ![gif](giphy|Kcw5bmss1LxiIYylkG)



## 5. What is the most ridiculous but practical accessory ever shown in a movie ?

**Score:** 363
**Body:** The golf umbrella hat in Space Jam . 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://preview.redd.it/0obz6vgb254h1.jpeg?width=676&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=d7b25907c10fd6ebf4e0b9122b337755c3a3005e
2. ![gif](giphy|40d1Z4ZkY9Ysg)
  - Single-handedly boosted the sales significantly of the swingline stapler with this movie. The prop wasn’t even made by swingline either.
3. https://preview.redd.it/lgt32fvyy44h1.jpeg?width=447&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=5e2ffdae46e4a98ed87db25d8018ee2ba1903398
  - We'll just tell your mother that... we ate it all.
4. https://preview.redd.it/hclpfbxn054h1.jpeg?width=645&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=34c5e39be62b56a64866910be8d69dd141cbb7e9


5. https://i.redd.it/7imd8q84354h1.gif
  - Not enough people know how truly amazing this movie is.
6. ![gif](giphy|3o6fJdOA9tiT1LRbZC)
7. https://preview.redd.it/wl24og1m054h1.png?width=1290&format=png&auto=webp&s=8eab903fd8573302755cf181817532ab9d61149a

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Thoughts on VIDEODROME (1982), directed by David Cronenberg

**Score:** 43
**Body:** It might be up there with my favorite one or two movies by this filmmaker.

I'm taking a class on Cronenberg in which VIDEODROME (1983--Sorry I erred on the year in the header) was the most recent viewing. It's unfortunate that my inaugural watch was on the night before class, so when the instructor asked us all to share our thoughts and impressions, I was still in the process of constructing mine.

Now that another day or so has passed, what I wish I'd said is that this film, to me, is a commentary on human nature when it comes to addiction; specifically, how it can lead us spiraling to our doom as we chase ever more potent highs.

That certainly seems the case for the characters of Max Renn (played by James Woods), who is addicted to watching sex and violence; and Nicki Brand (Debbie Harry), who seems addicted to pain.

But with these two protagonists, I think Cronenberg is trying to say there is a difference between someone whose excitement comes from watching and one compelled to experience something for real, Max being the former and Nicky the latter. For all the flak Max takes early on for being a connoisseur and purveyor of pornography, there is at least a point at which he recognizes the destructive nature of Videodrome. As such, he'll only go so far in pursuit of it.

Not so with Nicky, whose addiction overrides her sense of self-preservation, leading her to become a willing participant in her own degradation and murder.

So to all you kids out there whose parents give you grief for watching excessively violent or sexualized movies or TV series, next time tell them, "At least I'm just a viewer. I could actually be running around doing these things, or having them done to me, for real."

What I also liked about VIDEODROME: It's brutal and nihilistic, but not unlike some of the best film noir, at least insofar as I understand the genre. Max Renn may not be a detective per se, but not like Jake in CHINATOWN (1974), he navigates a sleazy underworld and thinks he's seen it all. He also gradually uncovers a conspiracy involving parties considerably more powerful and ruthless than him.

Instead of getting his nose sliced, Max gets sentient videotapes (Or hallucinations of them?) shoved into a pulsating yonic cavity in his abdomen. But perhaps most tragically, he is also helpless as the woman he loves is killed by those aforementioned unsavory forces. At least the universe Max exists in, as bleak as it is, still allows him some payback before the end credits roll.

I actually think that Cronenberg's decision to kill Nicky was smart and vital, because for last third or so of VIDEODROME, it gives Max motivation beyond getting to the bottom of a shadowy conspiracy. That's cerebral stuff whereas revenge is, I would argue, something more visceral.

It's actually kind of interesting that VIDEODROME has been described to me as a film full of ideas, but I found it genuinely affecting in my guts. The ooey-gooey special effects of the body mutations simultaneously repulsed and fascinated me while the revenge aspect compelled me across the finish line.

Long live the new flesh.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. What I find so fascinating about *Videodrome* is that it’s a film that is far, FAR ahead of its time, technologically.

The film deals with video tapes and there’s even a scene I recall from early in the film where we’re in Woods’ (or some other character’s) apartment and you see a box for the Atari VCS (what would later be the 2600) video game system… and it makes you realize just how old the technology was at that point versus now.

This is a movie that grasps for a technology that *does not yet exist* but uses the then current technology to make its story.  Had it been done a few years later, instead of VHS tapes we would be dealing with cyberpunk concepts, of the internet and being somehow hooked into it, something that William Gibson would do a few years later in his novels.

Despite this, that astonishes me all the more about this film, that it reaches for a technology that doesn’t exist and already sees the danger in it.  An incredible work!
  - I feel Existenz was an update of the ideas that went into videodrome, but with a more realistic technology, virtual reality gaming, to illustrate them and make the points it wanted to make.

It's been a long time since I have seen either, but I preferred Existenz. That may be because it starred my favourite actress though.
    - *Existenz*, IMHO, is one of those films that starts *really* strong but has a very weak conclusion/last chapter.

It wouldn’t shock me if Cronenberg took elements of *Videodrome* when he made that film and, yes, by that time he had the technology that as I noted in my OP wasn’t there for *Videodrome*… even as he did well with what he had!

I feel, in the end, that *Videodrome* is the better film.  Feels more gritty and “dirty”, and I kinda like that era of Cronenberg films with their science fiction/horror concepts a little more than what came later on.

That’s just IMHO of course!
      - Agreed. I always felt like Existenz runs out of steam 2/3 of the way through and lacks the hardcore commitment to its ideas that Videodrome has.
    - I saw Existenz in the theatre and am looking forward to watching it again. I didn't love it the first time, but it was also the first Cronenberg movie I ever watched, so maybe my brain just wasn't ready.
2. What I was most impressed by on my most recent watch was some of the ideas said in the first half of the movie. Maybe even the first 20s.  

Our very first scene Civic TV the one you take to bed. That is exactly how we are with are cellphones. We take it to bed with us and use it first thing in the morning.  

Then the interview with the 3 main characters has some excellent quotes.

Brian O'Blivion: "The battle for the mind of North America will be fought in the video arena: the Videodrome. The television screen is the retina of the mind's eye. Therefore, the television screen is part of the physical structure of the brain. Therefore, whatever appears on the television screen emerges as raw experience for those who watch it. Therefore, television is reality, and reality is less than television.


After all, there is nothing real outside our perception of reality, is there? "

Just like our smart phones and the media we consume is our reality. Whether your in a conservative, liberal, or somewhere in between echo chamber the media you consume is your eye into the world around you.  The media you consume is your reality.  Also the last line if we don't perceive it isn't real, so if our media doesn't tell use about it it doesn't exist.  So if my media circle tells me what the current administration is doing is good I'll believe it's good, if my media tells me it's bad than my reality is that it's bad.  I think this is the reason billionaires are buying up news organizations, they can shape our realaties. 

Niki Brand nails modern day smart phone addiction.

 Nicki Brand: "Well, I think we live in overstimulated times. We crave stimulation for its own sake. We gorge ourselves on it. We always want more, whether it's tactile, emotional or sexual. And I think that's bad....

I admit it. I live in a highly excited state of overstimulation."

Our constant search for our next dopamine hit from our phones, the doom scrolling, the social media post, the short clips, the memes. It's non-stop and we want stimulation just to be stimulated.  How many of use can't even walk around town, the store, work, or the park without need to check out phone for another dopamine hit?


These where the thoughts that really stuck out to me from my most recent viewing.  I first watched the movie 3 years ago and I have scene it 3 times.  It's one of my top 4 movies and my favorite Croneberg.  I have listened to the directors commentary and one with the actors too, really insightful behind the scenes stuff.

One thing I remember Croneberg mentioned was the phrase "The medium is the message." It's a quote from Marshall McLuhan. The idea that the content isn't what is important but how it's delivered.


3. Videodrome is definitely a film full of ideas — embodied in, well, bodies and their relation to technology and media.  The biggest idea, which Cronenberg talks explicitly about a lot in his commentary track, is "the medium is the message" from Marshall McLuhan, a Canadian like Cronenberg, btw.  The media we consume does not just give us “content.” It changes us. It shapes perception, desire, identity, even the body.  This is why the idea that “at least I’m only watching it instead of doing it” is off the mark of what Cronenberg is doing.  Max basically expresses that same idea in his tv interview: Civic TV gives people an outlet so they don’t have to act things out in real life.  In the commentary, Cronenberg basically says this is superficial and a cop-out.

The idea that media shapes us and our reality is also why the movie keeps blurring lines between what is “real,” what is Max’s delusion or hallucination, and what may or may not be media fabrication.  The media-induced uncertainty, ambiguity, and detachment from reality are not just plot devices but a key idea that the film is about.  And these issues are even more relevant today than they were when the film was made.
4. I haven't revisited Videodrome in a long time, even though I consider it one of my favorite Cronenberg films. 

I agree with another poster that Existenz touches on some of the same ideas, but I would like to bring Crimes of the Future into the equation, as a more modern take on a script whose origins are still old school Cronenberg. 

It has body horror and obsession as primary themes, but also looks at evolution and how people deal with change, and also about art as performance vs something else, and it also adds some self aware humor.

I'm not sure how connected you are to Toronto, but the station in Videodrome references CityTV and its blue movies, headed by someone named Moses.
5. I know this movie is very respected, and I will sound uncultured but I didn't understand the story while watching it, and later I forgot everything that happened in it despite having watched it only a few years ago (I usually remember plots). I remember that it looked cool, it had a special atmosphere, I was also very interested in it, but I didn't get drawn into the story. I know not everything is about the story, but things were happening and it was hard to follow them, I don't commonly have that problem where everything needs to be spoonfed. 
People accuse you all the time of "not getting" the movie just because you don't like it, this is one of the very few cases where I can really say that not only I didn't get the movie, even after reading about it after the story totally evaporated from my mind. 

I had this problem with the last Cronenmberg movie too, Crimes of the Future, for the first 15-20 min scenes were lining up one after the other without offering me any understanding of what I'm watching, I stopped because my attention started to radically drop no matter how intriguing the concepts may sound like in isolation. I realized that while a mystery can be a good hook to grab interest, if you just keep lining up one unusual thing after another without any thread the viewer can start to follow, it loses me. 

The Fly was great though. The Brood had a simple story, I wasn't crazy about it, but the grossness of it was well executed.
6. The film was very forward thinking and anticipated so much about our current era.  People living for/through the screen instead of real life, the worship of technology at the expense of the individual, the way corporations/media hides destructive things in benign or pleasurable products, and the way one extremity leads to another until annihilation is reached.

The film successfully avoids being moralistic, prudish, and sex phobic.  It's also so technically masterful, with the sure hand of a filmmaker knowing exactly what they want to say and how.  For me, it's easily one of Cronenberg's (and Woods) best work and time has made its message more potent, not less.

## 2. The Apprentice (2024) | The Devil Made A Deal With A Man And Lost

**Score:** 35
**Body:** This movie is like some kind of cursed painting you can’t look away from even though looking at it takes a toll on your sanity. There’s this weird claustrophobia to it that might come from its up close and hand held cinematography or from its two central figures. Trump and Cohn are not people you can ever enjoy being with and this movie, just like Wolf of Wall Street, doesn’t give us the relief of watching immoral people through the eyes of some moral protagonist. We’re left to fend for ourselves.

Jeremy Strong’s Roy Cohn is the black hole around which the film revolves. He plays it like some kind of Eldritch being wearing a human skin suit that’s way too tight. It felt like a more grounded version of Vincent D'Onofrio in Men in Black. Had the movie taken a turn for the supernatural in the third act I would’ve bought it solely on the creepiness of Strong’s performance.

I’m not a huge fan of biopic acting or the discourse around it so I was pleasantly surprised by Sebastian Stan’s performance. I never felt like he was mimicking Trump. His performance reminds you of Trump while also being a unique character built for this story. Stan brings a surprising amount of humanity to this character without the movie ever humanising him. 

So it’s particularly jarring when Trump’s strange charisma (as portrayed in the movie) catches you off-guard. The movie does a great job of showing you why he has followers and why he desperately needs them.

Ali Abbasi and Gabriel Sherman portray Trump as a man who has a clawing need for respect that he feels was always denied to him. His father doesn’t give him any. No one in his social class gives him the time of day until Cohn takes him under his wing. Stan plays him as hunger personified. He wants everything and he feels that’s what America owes him. He sees himself as the embodiment of America so much so that when he says America isn’t respected it really means he isn’t. No one’s more disrespected than him and no one deserves respect more.

The ending shot of New York and the American flag reflected in Trump’s eyes is great. Sure, it’s a bit on the nose but I thought the film became less and less subtle as its main character evolved into his final form. So to end it on such a shot that both conveys character and shouts, “look at my directorial flair,” felt appropriate.

I’d be remiss if I didn’t mention Maria Bakalova who brings a lot of character in her short screen time. 

PS: One of the more subtle bits of writing that I really liked was how the word democracy itself is used as a dog whistle for oligarchy. Subtle in the sense that the movie doesn’t call attention to it but not in the sense that it takes a couple of viewings to figure it out. 

I can’t imagine watching this a second time.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think you nailed that. I enjoy both leads but I had very low expectations for this films (I’m not generally a fan of biopics, especially of very recent figures) but this really impressed me.

## 3. Rise of 'viral' movies

**Score:** 3
**Body:** I'm noticing with the fall of Marvel that there hasn't been until the last few years, viral mono-culture films that everyone will see no matter the interest level or if it's good or bad. Marvel seemed like a very different kind of hype before it died down, true absurd levels of fandom and general interest. 

But now, the rise of YouTube filmmakers is somehow taking the full force of online fandom and bringing it to the cinema and the combined interest feels very different than anything I've seen in a long long time. 

The first sort of viral movie that got hyped up to a fever pitch was everything everywhere all at once. Films like Sinners, Talk to me, Obsession, Backrooms all have this intense virality to them. That despite being good or bad, there's this, let's all embrace this film no matter what, quality to them. 

The most recent Backrooms and Obsession films had me very interested in this phenomenon as of late. They were pretty good overall and entertaining enough albeit some issues here and there. 

But the hype that comes with YouTube creator "ip" as it were, is unreal. What do you guys think? Are we going to see continuous waves of viral film hype every year now? 

I think it's good for the film industry but I am scratching my head now every time I watch one. The intensity of the fandom is actually more interesting than the movies themselves but that doesn't mean they're bad or anything

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Eh, the landscape’s so subjective though. I saw one Avengers movie, and most folks I know didn’t catch any of em. I’m hopeful for Backrooms, but my decade of video store experience taught me that (in a micro level, anyways) horror fans watch horror movies, diehard horror fans watch any horror movies, people who don’t watch horror *never* watch horror, Black people are starving for Black-led movies, tween girls are fucked, and people who watch whatever’s popular… aren’t so homogenous. Some folks rented Devil Wears Prada because it was big, but straight dudes largely didn’t. Lots of people rented Without a Paddle or Iron Man, but nobody I ever sustained a conversation with. 2. > viral mono-culture films that everyone will see no matter the interest level or if it's good or bad.

Most people don't think of films as good or bad, as much as that may surprise you. They go to the film to see what it has to offer, to decide if they like it. My barber is like that. He never hates movies, he just likes the cool parts, and some movies have a ton of cool parts. For him it's more like baseball, there are a lot of pitches, he enjoys the hits.

Films have always been talked up to legendary status by people. In the 70s and 80s it was kids on the playground. Everyone knew that Jaws was coming out and the hype was intense even though there was no internet, no cable TV, just ads in the newspaper and word-of-mouth. 

There's always been viral hype. It was a meme back then, and a meme now. (Not the picture memes, the broader sort of meme, when the word was coined in 1976.)

Why *wouldn't* the industry invest low amounts of money on something with a built-in audience?
  - I agree on traditional hype of blockbuster fair and other films throughout history. I've lived through many of these moments and remember them fondly.

I think modern virality is a different beast though. It's like a meme that hits so perfectly, so aptly in how it describes the current climate / situation that everyone shares it and becomes a piece of monoculture.

In some way, it almost feels like there's a sector of films that are similar to memes in a way. They're like reactions to something that we all see and know about and we embrace it because it's a reflection of a feeling / sentiment and not just a traditional story
    - I’d say look into the marketing for these films and the demographics they targeted online. Marketing has gotten very incognito through social media with bots, influencers, ads, etc. But also, any film and person behind it that makes a certain demographic/generation feel seen will spark something. It’s great for the industry
3. I don’t see whats wrong with these “viral” movies.

The Substance, get out, barbarian, weapons, sinners, and yeah we got backrooms and obsession. Obsession was so great. Backrooms was hardly horror. 

With backrooms, I’m seeing the emergence of a new sort of new genre of just like unsettling and psychedelic and surreal. Something that fits in with time-loop stuff. Time-loop, Truman show stuff, ghost in a romance movies, and the latest boots Riley movies, these are things that have been hard to place. I think the studios are making a bet and marketing movies like these pretty hard because it’s not something that you would necessarily know to seek out. Backrooms had a 10mil budget and they put their faith into a kid. It was awesome. Not a lot of movies like that. 

I don’t see what the big deal is really. A24 is making bets. The people making these bets go to film festivals. They go to Cannes. The general public has an appetite for film festival films especially for like these highly competitive genre film stuffs from people who may not have had that much money to make their first videos and proof of concept shorts. 

Sundance and TIFF have become what they are, we get films like coda and Anora and whatnot. But i for one am glad that we are seeing hype trains for new filmmakers. Is it always going to be PTA and Wes Anderson and Christopher Nolan and then reused IP over and over? 

It’s just a matter of trying to hype up stuff that they’re really betting on being popular, because they saw it first, they invested heavily in it, when people see it, it spreads like wildfire. Awards shows, too, horror and genre films are going more towards this sort of thing. 



## 4. Are there any films that we could honestly say are widely beloved by both serious cinephile-types and the general public alike?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I’m basically asking whether there has ever been a film that is considered to be of high substance, as decided by the cultural elite, that also entertains (or perhaps provokes a sincere response from) the average Joe.

My only guess is the classic one that always gets brought up in this conversation (and is unsurprisingly the offering Reddit AI gave when I looked to see if anyone had ever asked a question like this before): The Godfather. But even then, I’ve met plenty of cinephiles who find it slow and indulgent (“it insists upon itself” after all) and I could definitely find everyday people who find it boring.

I’m conscious that there are also plenty of popular films that required widely respected craft/innovative filmmaking techniques. (Star Wars or The Wizard of Oz, for example, are generally highly regarded in history of cinema courses.) But when I ask about films that are beloved by the more elitist cinephiles types, imagine a pretentious circle of hoity-toity types sipping aperol at Cannes. I mean films that people like that would be proud to put in their top 5 and not just because it has a personal, nostalgic quality, but because they think it is actually “Good” the way a Fellini or Kurosawa film is good.

I’m also aware of the few blockbustery type movies on the Criterion collection, but as I understand that, that was because of some weird licensing thing right? Might not be the best thing to to off.

Perhaps the deeper question is, can art of any kind pull this off? I’d personally be interested to know if there are any novels, paintings, albums or poems that fall into this category.

Edit: spelling and grammar

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Miyazaki films likely fit into the overlap here. Films like My Neighbor Totoro and Princess Mononoke are beloved by basically every potential audience. 

Films like Godfather probably get there, or some of the Scorsese films that have popular overlap - but tbh it feels like popular audiences are moving away from long form dramas and I'm not so sure these types of films would be nearly as popular if they were to come out today. 
2. I mean you bring up a question of what type of “cinephile” you’re talking about. That bell curve meme about enjoying movies is actually pretty accurate. I would say most people would recognize a lot of the works of Spielberg as masterpieces. You’ll get some snooty people who say no, but push pass that further into cinephilia and you can find the people who have picked up the Cahiers du Cinema tradition of finding art in the mainstream. There’s always going to be some people who claim to dislike any movie the general public like, but I don’t think they’re worth talking to much anyway.
3. Oh plenty. While there are certainly films that sit in either extreme, I think there’s a very healthy overlap of well-made crowd-pleasers. I think they tend to be older films, either because it takes time for them to establish cult popularity or because they’re under-recognized critically until they have some resurgence, but you can look through many of the still-popular movies from the 80’s, for example, and find plenty.

For a more recent example (though still not “new”), take something like *Fury Road*, which is an incredible thrill-ride, but also masterfully executed. 
4. General audience taste may be narrowing, but the snobby “cinephile” stereotypes you’re invoking, if they were ever substantively real, are fifty years out of date. Check out the composition of some actual recent Cannes juries. In the past five years we’ve had Demi Moore, Park Chan-wook, Halle Berry, Greta Gerwig, Brie Larson, Spike Lee: popular figures of entertainment cinema who care about movies and whom are beloved by the “hoity-toity” festival audience in turn.

Parasite won the Palme D’or and went on to make a quarter of a billion dollars. Get Out was a huge hit among normie horror fans and is also on the Sight and Sound “Best Movies Of All Time” list. (If you sort that list by “Popular” on Letterboxd, you get: Parasite, Get Out, Spirited Away, The Shining, and then The Godfather.)

You can always find people who think they’re too good for a particular film, or who think it’s boring, but this line you’re drawing isn’t as firm as you think it is.
5. The Wizard of Oz is highly regarded in cinema courses????

I'm glad you told me to imagine a pretentious circle of hoity-toity types at Cannes, because that's just what this scenario is - in your imagination. There isn't this lofty cabal of cultural elite who pronounce from on high what films are good or bad. There isn't this massive divide between cinema for the masses and cinema for the elite. The same people who loved Palme D'Or winners like Anatomy of a Fall or It Was Just an Accident also love things like Heated Rivalry or RuPaul's Drag Race. People who truly love art love all art, as long as it connects with them.

You say the elite look to people like Fellini and Kurosawa. My man, have you SEEN a Kurosawa film? He was ALL ABOUT the mass audience! Seven Samurai is the blueprint for many summer blockbusters, Hidden Fortress was the ur-Star Wars, this was a guy making popular entertainment whose direct successors are George Lucas and Steven Spielberg, the two most popularity-driven filmmakers on the planet. And Spielberg is BELOVED at Cannes. The French New Wave filmmakers who basically started this stereotype of the pretentious cinephile, their favorite Hollywood filmmaker was Alfred Hitchcock, who was singularly focused on box office numbers and thrilling the masses. They also loved the work of Howard Hawks and John Ford, two people who couldn't give two shits about whether their films played at festivals.

Your question of whether "any art can pull this off" is wild. All art does this all the time. The Beatles and the Beach Boys did this! Andy Warhol did this! Banksy is doing this right now, in our time! Bjork is one of the most popular artists on the planet! There are also literally massive volumes of scholarship on the way art transcends artificial barriers of "high" and "low" in our society. It is one of the defining characteristics of the rise of postmodernism, that the cultural understanding of "high art" vs "low art" has collapsed.

What this tells me is that you need to familiarize yourself with more films, more art, and read more about the history of modernism and postmodernism in the 20th century. Move beyond these cultural stereotypes of High vs Low, elite vs. the masses. You've built this world in your head that does not exist and the discourse has already moved past this decades ago. The cultural elite love a great audience-forward barnburner as much as the rest of us, and your uncle Cletus who lives in Bumfuck North Dakota might sob when his wife takes him to see Hamnet. Art is art, yo. It contains multitudes. And so do people.
6. I’d say most of Villeneuve’s films fall into this bucket. He sits at the intersection of blockbuster and arthouse.  

Cinephiles and film bros alike can appreciate a movie like Sicario. It has enough depth to be more than shallow entertainment, and enough action/suspense to satisfy a casual viewer.
7. I think the way you are qualifying types makes it impossible to answer the question.

So it goes.

9. Ugh I hate this take on the Godfather. “It insists upon itself” is just a joke from Family Guy. It is not an actual legit criticism and honestly makes no sense. The Godfather is based on a book by Mario Puzo, so even if you were to make that claim (which no serious critic has ever put forth) you’d really have to criticize the book, which actually is fair because the book goes on a lot of weird tangents that have very little to do with the main plot. The movie eliminates every useless side plot though. 

There really is nothing in the movie that “insists upon itself.” There is so little suspension of disbelief, so little pretentiousness, so little wrong with the movie that I believe anyone who earnestly puts forward this take only does so to be contrarian and probably hasn’t even seen the film. 

You don’t need to like it, you can say it’s pacing is not for you and you don’t like the characters, but come on, don’t just regurgitate Peter Griffin quotes and act like it’s serious or even relevant criticism.
